content:
Ryan, I’d do two things—shepherd my child in the moment, and speak with the elder later.

With my 10-year-old when Psalm 19 is read:
- I’d squeeze his hand and whisper, “Listen—God really made all this, and the skies really do tell us about Him. And God’s Word is perfect. Poetry uses pictures, but it tells the truth.”
- After the service, I’d open Psalm 19 and show him both halves: creation declares God’s glory (with images like “a tent for the sun”), and God’s law is perfect and trustworthy. I’d explain that saying “the sun runs its course” is like us saying “the sun sets”—it’s true from our vantage point and it’s poetic, not make-believe.
- Then I’d say, “Some parts of the Bible are poetry and some are history; all of it is true. Genesis 1 has artistry, but it’s still telling us what God actually did: He created everything by His word. Good Christians disagree on how to understand the word ‘day,’ but we don’t call something ‘poetry’ just to avoid hard conversations. We ask, ‘What did God intend to say?’ and we trust Him because His Word is perfect.”

How I’d handle the elder’s comment:
- I’d meet privately and respectfully: “Brother, can you clarify what you mean by ‘read Genesis 1 as poetic’? Do you mean recognizing literary beauty, or setting aside its historical claims for the sake of peace?”
- I’d affirm unity and inerrancy: “Unity is precious, but it rests on truth. Psalm 19 says God’s Word is perfect. Let’s teach genre carefully without implying that ‘poetry’ equals ‘less true.’”
- I’d ask us to protect consciences: “Can we agree on core creation truths—God made all things by His Word, Adam and Eve were real, humanity fell—and allow charitable discussion on the length of the days, without pressuring families to adopt a label that unsettles them?”
- I’d commit to charity: “I won’t make this a hobbyhorse, and I won’t undermine you publicly. But I also can’t teach my child that we adjust our reading of Scripture to keep the peace.”

In short, with my child I’d use Psalm 19 to build confidence that poetry tells real truth and that all Scripture is trustworthy. With the elder, I’d pursue unity anchored in the truth, not unity purchased by blurring what God has said.
